04NHI INTELLIGENCE INSIGHTS

Patterns only visible through machine analysis of the full dataset simultaneously

1. PRICE-SIZE POWER LAW (R² = 0.90)

Price scales as SqFt1.18 — every doubling in size yields a 2.3x price increase. Properties deviating >20% from this curve are anomalies. The model identified 7 undervalued and 5 overvalued listings the market hasn't corrected.

2. BIMODAL MARKET — TWO HIDDEN SUBMARKETS

$/sqft density reveals three peaks at $303, $455, and $844 per sqft. The market isn't a single continuum — it's two distinct submarkets with different dynamics. The Value Corridor (<$379/sf, 13 listings) averages 77 days on market, while the Premium Tier (>$379/sf, 85 listings) averages 67 days. The premium tier is actually MORE oversupplied despite appearing stronger.

3. BUILDING CONCENTRATION RISK

8 buildings have 3+ simultaneous listings — an institutional selling signal. 3001 Veazey Terrace leads with 7 units listed at once (73% price spread). 2939 Van Ness has 4 units averaging 134 days on market. When multiple units list simultaneously, buyers gain significant negotiation leverage.

4. HOA-ADJUSTED TRUE COST

HOA fees represent 20-44% of total monthly housing cost in this market. The cheapest true cost properties (mortgage + tax + HOA per sqft) are at 3001 Veazey at $2.59/sf/month. The most expensive reach $7.20/sf/month. HOA-blind comparison leads buyers to systematically overpay by ignoring this 20-44% hidden burden.

5. VINTAGE ARBITRAGE — 45% DISCOUNT

The year-built to $/sqft relationship is nonlinear with a cubic fit. Mid-century buildings (1945-1970) trade at ~$428/sf vs modern (2001+) at ~$779/sf — a 45% discount. Pre-war buildings (pre-1930) command a premium ($635/sf) driven by architectural character and larger lot sizes, creating a U-shaped price curve.

6. THE $1.2M BEDROOM CLIFF

The marginal cost of the 2BR→3BR jump is $1,245,000 — far exceeding the 1BR→2BR jump of $212K. This is the "family premium" — once you need 3 bedrooms, you're competing in a fundamentally different market tier (townhomes/SFH vs condos). The 4BR tier actually drops in $/sqft, offering better value per foot.

7. DOM CLUSTERING REVEALS AGENT STRATEGIES

Days-on-market clusters at 25, 44, 72, 109, 127, and 146 days — not random but reflecting systematic price reduction cycles. Clusters at ~25d and ~45d map to typical 1st and 2nd price drops. The 127-146d cluster represents listings that survived two agent contracts, signaling maximum seller fatigue and negotiation opportunity.
